"You heard it too, didn't you?" Nora asked, lowering the kettle before it could sing over her words.

"The knock?" said Felix. "Not a knock. Three taps, then a pause, then two more. Whoever it is has manners."

Mara looked up from the ledger. "Or a code."

"Everything is a code to you," Felix replied, though he smiled when he said it.

"Everything important is," Mara said. She closed the book with one finger between the pages, as if the sentence inside might still be useful later. "If it were the baker, he would call out. If it were my brother, he would come through the yard and complain about the gate. If it were trouble, it would not wait politely."

Nora tilted her head. "That leaves what?"

"News," Mara said.

Felix crossed to the window, lifted the curtain no more than an inch, and let it fall again. "A woman in a green coat. Hat pinned down tight. She looks like the sort of messenger who has already walked through two wrong storms and has no appetite for a third."

"Then open the door," Nora said. "No one should have to stand in weather like that while we debate the philosophy of knocking."

"You say that now," Felix answered, drawing the bolt, "but if this turns out to be a tax collector with a beautiful vocabulary, I intend to remind you of your generosity."

When the door opened, the woman outside did not step forward at once. "Mara Vale?" she asked.

"I am Mara," Mara said from the table.

"Good," the visitor replied. Her voice was hoarse, but steady. "I was told that if I found the room with the blue kettle, the woman who keeps the quiet books would know what to do next."
